content: Hello, did you always keep all your incoming messages in the same folder Inbox or did you move or archive messages to other folders? Is this an Inbox folder in a POP or an IMAP account? Which is the size of the Inbox folder and how many messages does it contain? R-click on Inbox > Properties > number of messages & size on disk.... Do you have a backup copy of your Thunderbird profile folder? Did you change your retention policy last year? Account Settings > account name > Synchronisation and Storage > Disk Space...

Hi and many thanks for replying. I kept/ stored most emails in inbox (just a few in other folders). Did like this in the past 8 plus years and had never problems. I also got no advice if there is a certain limit to keep mails in inbox. Is this the case?

It's IMAP.

Right now it contains 4587 messages and takes 452 MB. I guess it had way more before the lost.

I don't know if Thunderbird did backups automatically. Maybe my fault I didn't consider doing it manually.

There is no space limit for storing. Inbox offline reading was/ is also activated for offline reading. 'Deleting messages when in need for space' was on 'no messages delete'.
creator: christophleib

content:
You certainly didn't change the default mailbox (also called mbox) storage format, which means that all your messages have been stored in one text file names Inbox. And unfortunately an Inbox files gets bigger and bigger if downloaded messages are not regularly archived or moved to other folders, and it might get corrupt. Thunderbird doesn't make automatic profile backups, it's up to the user to save his data.

Nevertheless verify the size of the INBOX file in Thunderbird's profile folder: go to Help > Troubleshooting Information > Profile Folder > Open Folder (if you are on Mac: Show in Finder). In the profile folder open the ImapMail folder containing then an "imap.xxx.xx" account folder and look up the size of the INBOX file (the one without suffixe). Also ask your email address provider if the have a backup of your account.

content:
For any program 'Full Screen' is Full Screen and Task bar is always not visible.

On Windows 11, I can use 'F11' key to go to full screen and use again to come out of Full Screen.

At the top of each window that is not full screen, there are three icons on top right. The one in the middle is 'maximise' Use that option to 'maximise' the window so it uses all the desktop, but it does not cover the Task Bar.

I presume you have a Thunderbird shortcut icon on desktop. Check it has correct settings.

  • Right click on desktop Thunderbird icon and select 'Properties'
  • In the 'Shortcut' tab
  • Run: ideally set at 'Normal Window' as it gives you options after starting OR if you always want it maximised then select 'Maximised'
  • Click on 'Apply' and click on 'OK'

content:
re :For you setting the shortcut to run in different modes changes anything in start behavior of Thunderbird?

Yes. If Run: 'maximised' Then Thunderbird starts up with a maximised window using all available screen.

If Run: 'minimised' Then Thunderbird will start as 'minimised' and display icon on TaskBar. If I need to then use it, a click on that icon on TaskBar will show the Thunderbird window.

If Run: Normal Window Then Thunderbird will start up in a window of height and width set by me. It might look like it filles the whole screen if that was something previously set up, but the difference is that I can hover over the side edge or top edge, cursor changes to a double head arrow, left click-hold down and then drag the edge to a suitable size. Eg: Window now uses about half the screen size. That means if I maximise using the middle icon, it fills screen and then I can use the same middle icon to 'Restore down' back to normal window size.

content:
I'm using Windows 11 OS and it's up to date. I have to admit I've never used Snap, so I altered settings to check. I've enabled Snap. I hide the Task Bar. I Maximised Thunderbird and it used entire visible screen. I hover over bottom of screen - TaskBar pops up and remains visible providing I continue to hover cursor over TaskBar.

Mmmmmm?! I've been testing things to see if I can get same issue as you.

I disabled snap and tried again. This time I managed to get the error you are talking about and it remains even after enabling snap again.

So I restored down Thunderbird to a normal sized window and moved window so the bottom was at bottom of screen. Interesting - If I hover over bottom of screen where Thunderbird is displaying - no pop up of Task Bar, but if move to where it's not covering bottom edge - pop up of Task Bar appears.

I then selected to open any folder stored on computer - maximised it and hover cursor over bottom of screen and yes I still have the same issue, so I can get the no pop up of Taskbar using any program. I also tested with Edge - same error. This issue is not Thunderbird or Firefox - it's something odd in Windows OS.

Still testing various things.

Re opened the Multitasking in Windows and disabled snap. Closed that window. Not closing it did not seem to get the Taskbar back. So, it's important to change a setting and close window. Restore down windows to hover over bottom of screen and select 'Taskbar Settings' Enabled Taskbar view again and closed. Then open Taskbar Settings and hide the Taskbar

Guess what.... After maximising various windows and testing - in all cases hover over the bottom edge of screen got it to pop up again. Even after enabling snap again.

This problem you are experiencing is not Thunderbird - it's Windows OS. It clearly has a glitch. As I managed to create the problem and remove the problem by only enabling and disabling the Snap feature and the Taskbar hide feature.

The main issue is trying to find out how to force this Windows error to occur with specific steps.

re :Weird that for you setting the shortcut to start Thunderbird minimized works. For me and many others who use Thunderbird do not work and need to use an add-on to do this.

It's Windows that controls the shortcut icon on desktop not Thunderbird.

content:
nndegroote said
I'm not familiar with thunderbird but am using firefox on two laptops. On one it will auto hide but on the other it will not. I previously set it to autohide in firefox settings but can't find the option on the second laptop. Any help please. (By auto hide I mean it's not there until you hold the mouse over it.)

re :I'm not familiar with thunderbird You are aware that you are specifically on a Thunderbird forum and we try to help issues that are specific to the program itself.

The autohide of Taskbar is not a Thunderbird email client nor Firefox browser setting.

It's a setting on your computer. For queries regarding how to use your computer eg: Windows OS, you should be asking on a Microsoft or Windows forum. But on this occasion, I've added some info below for you. You have a 'Task bar' and it's usually positioned across the bottom of the screen / monitor.

  • Right click on Taskbar and select 'Taskbar Settings'.
  • There should be an option 'Taskbar behaviours' and it will offer the option/checkbox to 'Automatically hide the taskbar'.

Alternative:

  • In Windows search type: 'Settings'
  • select: 'Settings'
  • Select 'Personalisation'
  • Select 'Taskbar'
  • There should be an option 'Taskbar behaviours' and it will offer the option/checkbox to 'Automatically hide the taskbar'.

content:
Do you want to create a new account to access your gmail emails ?

If yes:

Gmail uses Oauth2, so you need to make sure a few things are set up correctly first. Firewall: Access Firewall - it might be the defaulton computer or controlled by an Anti-Virus prgram. Set up Thunderbird program as 'allowed' so nothing gets blocked. Confirm Thunderbird is set up as allowed program.

VPN: Check computer for a VPN. Some Anti-Virus products now offer a VPN and I have come across some peopl who did not realise one had been enabled. Switch off VPN (or create split tunnelling and set it up for Thunderbird to use). Gmail does not like VPN as it atempts to hide your IP address, so gmail decides you might be hacker so it auto blocks access. Confirm VPN is switched off

Localhost: Do you have anything using the localhost? People who manage websites may typically use eg: Apache Xampp. You need to switch of any program using the localhost whilst setting up gmail. You can enable it again afterwards. I had to switch off Apache Xampp. Confirm nothing is using localhost.

In Thunderbird

  • Settings > Privacy & Security
  • Web Content
  • Select 'Accept cookies from sites'
  • Click on 'Exceptions' button
  • Enter this : https://accounts.google.com
  • Click on 'Allow
  • Click on 'Save Changes'

IF you previously had the gmail account and are reinstalling it then you need to clear the passwords

  • Whilst in that area - scroll to Passwords section
  • click on 'Saved Passwords'
  • If you see any mention of the account you are trying to set up - select the relevant lines and click on 'Remove'
  • Click on Close


Switch off the Account Hub as it's still experimental.

  • Settings > General
  • Account Hub
  • uncheck this checkbox: 'Create accounts in the new Account Hub'

Then create the mail account

  • Menu app > New Account > Email
  • Enter name
  • Enter gmail email address
  • Enter password
  • Select checkbox to remember password.
  • Click on 'Continue'

Thunderbird by default will look for the IMAP server settings. They should be:

  • Server name: imap.gmail.com
  • Port: 993
  • Connection Security: SSL/TLS
  • Authentication Method: OAuth2
  • Username = full gmail email address
  • Password is the same one you use to access webmail

Outgoing SMTP:

  • Server name: smtp.gmail.com
  • Port: 465
  • Connection Security: SSL/TLS
  • Authentication Method: OAuth2
  • Username = full gmail email address
  • Password is the same one you use to access webmail
  • Assuming all is ok click on 'Done'

You should then get the gmail pop up browser type window. It will ask you to confirm you really are you and the then askif you want to allow Thunderbird access to server.

Then an OAuth token gets created and stored in the same place as all other passwords.
creator: Toad-Hall

content:
Passwords can only be created or modified etc via the webmail account for that email address which you access/logon via a browser.

Once the new password has been set up, you then update the stored password in Thunderbird.

  • Settings > Privacy & Security
  • Scroll to Passwords section
  • click on 'Saved Passwords
  • click on 'Show Passwords'

Accounts usually have two entries one for incoming and one for outgoing. Username is useful as it usually is the email address. Pop starts with 'mailbox://' Imap starts with 'imap://' Smtp start with 'smtp://' Some accounts use Oauth, so will see a line 'oauth://'

  • Right click on relevant line and select 'Edit Password'
  • Completely clear all contents
  • Carefully type in the correct password

You need to do this for all the relevant line for the account/username

  • click on 'Close'

Exit Thunderbird, allow a few seconds for background processes to complete and then restart Thunderbird.
creator: Toad-Hall

content: If this is an IMAP account, Thunderbird saves the draft periodically. If the time to write the message is longer than the time to save a draft, thunderbird saves it again. If you take longer time to write the message, thunderbird continues periodically to save more drafts. when the message is finally sent, thunderbird issues the command to delete the draft, but can cause the other drafts to remain. You have to manually delete those. OR, you can set the drafts folder to be local, in which case this situation never materializes.
creator: davidsk

content:
1. Sent

The settings you have set up are correct for a Pop account. You should not be needing to alter anything.

Possible reasons for a setting not being recognised. You have a program on your computer that cleans up temp files, session files, cache etc. Products such as CCleaner or WiseCleaner or even some Anti-Virus products can 'clean up' files. It means something could be messing about in places where they should not be allowed and it can cause a nuisance. If you have installed such a program then you need to access it and make an exception to not have access to your Thunderbird profile. That includes any Anti-Virus. Anti-Virus can cause problems because if allowed to scan and auto fix files, they could cause major data loss. AV's have no concept that one simple text file may contain more than one email. So a fix may mean delete the contents of file. If you have had Thunderbird for a while then it's likely the Thunderbird folder that needs being make exempt from scanning is here:

  • C:Users/username/Appdata/Roaming/Thunderbird


The 'Sent' folder does not display items because the index file does not match the real contents in the mbox file.

  • Right click on 'Sent' folder and select 'Properties'
  • Click on 'Repair Folder'
  • click on 'OK'

content:
Do you have any VPN running? Many servers do not like a VPN as it hides your IP address. Many Anti-Virus products now offer a VPN and in some cases I've discovered they were auto enabled. Please double check to see if a VPN was enabled - switch it off. If you discover a VPN then check for a Kill Switch - some VPN if connection is lost will invoke a Kill Switch which stops internet connection.

re :add my Spectrum email back in Thunderbird, I presume the lost Thunderbird meant you had to reinstall Thunderbird or when you started Thunderbird it had lost knowing how to access the mail account acting ike it was using a new profile.

Did you also remove all the previously saved passwords? If no then please check now and remove them if they still exist.

  • Settings > Privacy & Security
  • Scroll to Passwords section
  • click on 'Saved Passwords'

Do you have any lines where the 'username' = the email address of account you are trying to add back into Thunderbird ? Normally, each account would have two lines one for incoming and one for outoing. Select each relevant line and click on 'Remove'. Then Exit Thunderbird to force background processes to properly update file. Upon restart - try to create account.

Are you talking about a 'spectrum' email address or another that uses the spectrum server? Are you using a password that spectrum says is 'secure' ? Maybe Spectrum is being awkward because it wants you to set up a new password that they deem as more secure. eg: uses 12 chars, uses upper and lower case with numerals.

If sending an event to people who need to say they accept etc - the information about the event is usually made accessible via a link to a website page, so they can access the information.

content:
There is a possibility, but it's tedious. If you have not compacted the folders, try this:

  1. click help>troubleshootinginformation
  2. scroll to 'profile folders' and click 'open folder'
  3. exit thunderbird
  4. you are now in the profile. If the messages were in an IMAP account (that's my guess), click the imapmail folder and then the account folder. you should see the folder with the missing messages, such as INBOX, INBOX.msf. Ignore the msf files.
  5. Open the file "Inbox" with Notepad or a similar text editor. ("INBOX" has no suffix; do not open "INBOX.msf.") Locate your deleted email by searching the document.
  6. Locate the line before your email text that reads "X-Mozilla-Status: 000X" (where 'X' is any character other than 0). Change the number to four zeros (″0000"). Repeat for all other messages. Save the document and exit Notepad
  7. Now, COPY the file to the Mail\Local Folders folder in the profile
  8. restart thunderbird and the messages should appear.

content:
I find that such hanging is rare, which suggest to me that there's some local factor that makes it worse for you. Some things you might look into...

- Could be one of your add-ons is misbehaving. Try disabling them or running Thunderbird in Troubleshooting Mode to see if the problem goes away.

- Disable Hardware Acceleration in Thunderbird - it can sometimes contribute to display or performance anomalies.

- Try excluding your Thunderbird profile from your antivirus.

But to address your original question, You can End Task from Windows Task Manager. Right-click on an empty spot on the Toolbar to find it. There may be multiple Thunderbird processes.

On Windows 11, you can force-close unresponsive apps directly from the Taskbar using the End task option. However, this feature is disabled by default and must be turned on manually. To enable the “End Task” option from the Advanced page, follow these steps:

- Open Settings on Windows 11.

- Click on System.

- Click the Advanced page.

- Turn on the “End Task” toggle switch under the “Taskbar settings.

content:
If Firefox can't connect to their web mail site, that seems like a pretty clear indication that either they are having a problem or they have changed their server addresses. Does your email provider have a help/support page where you can check to see if the web mail site is up?

There are a few web site that can check to see if another site or domain is down. Google the phrase "is that web site down" and you'll find a few. The one I often use is called, "Is It Down Right Now."

Many ISPs that had provided email services are now subcontracting those services to third-party hosts. My ISP, Xfinity/Comcast, has announced that their email will be moving to Yahoo, though it hasn't happened yet. I don't find that these companies are necessarily very good at keeping their customers informed. I wonder if you may be caught up in the crossfire of one of these transitions?
creator: rednoise
